sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A series RC circuit contained a 6.0 V battery, a 10 kΩ resistor, and a 100 µF capacitor. The capacitor was initially uncharged, and the switch was closed at t = 0. The time constant is τ = RC = 1.0 s. Students recorded capacitor voltage over time and compared the data with VC(t) = V0(1 - e^-t/RC). They noted that after one time constant the capacitor voltage reached about 63% of its final value.
questions.stem: What is the time constant of the circuit?

explanations.A: Correct. τ = RC = (10,000 Ω)(100 × 10^-6 F) = 1.0 s. The time constant sets the timescale for charging and discharging.
explanations.B: 0.10 s would result from a factor-of-ten error in either resistance or capacitance.
explanations.C: 10 s would require a much larger R or C than given.
explanations.D: 100 s is two orders of magnitude too large for the stated values.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Capacitor voltage during charging of a 6.0 V RC circuit with τ = 1.0 s.

questions.stem: Approximately what is VC after one time constant?

explanations.C: Correct. After one τ, VC = V0(1 - e^-1) ≈ 0.63V0. For a 6.0 V battery, 0.63 × 6.0 ≈ 3.8 V. The capacitor approaches 6.0 V asymptotically rather than instantly.
explanations.A: 1.0 V confuses the time constant value with voltage.
explanations.B: 6.0 V is the final value approached after several time constants, not after one.
explanations.D: The capacitor begins at 0 V but charges substantially after one time constant.

questions.stem: What happens to the initial current when the switch is first closed?

choices.B: It is maximal because the uncharged capacitor initially behaves like a wire.
choices.A: It is zero because capacitors never allow current.
choices.C: It equals the final current after long charging.
choices.D: It flows from lower potential to higher potential through the resistor.

explanations.B: Correct. At t = 0, the capacitor has no stored charge and no voltage across it, so the resistor initially sees nearly the full battery voltage. Initial current is I0 = V/R and then decays as the capacitor charges.
explanations.A: In a DC charging transient, current flows initially even though steady-state current later goes to zero.
explanations.C: The final current approaches zero after the capacitor is charged.
explanations.D: Conventional current flows from higher to lower potential through the external resistor.

questions.stem: If capacitance were doubled while resistance stayed fixed, what would happen to τ?

choices.A: It would be cut in half.

explanations.D: Correct. The time constant is directly proportional to capacitance. Doubling C doubles RC and makes the circuit charge more slowly.
explanations.A: Halving would occur if capacitance were halved, not doubled.
explanations.B: τ depends on C, so it cannot remain unchanged.
explanations.C: Resistance and capacitance are positive quantities, so τ is positive.

questions.stem: After a long time, what is the current in the circuit?

explanations.B: Correct. After many time constants, the capacitor is essentially fully charged to the battery voltage. No steady DC current flows through an ideal capacitor in series, so current approaches zero.
explanations.A: V/R describes the initial current, not the long-time current.
explanations.C: There is no mechanism for infinite current in this resistor-containing circuit.
explanations.D: The source is a DC battery, not an AC supply.

questions.passage: A simplified cell membrane was modeled as a capacitor in parallel with ion-channel resistance. A patch had membrane capacitance 20 pF and leak resistance 500 MΩ. After a current pulse, the membrane potential relaxed back toward rest with time constant τ = RmCm. In a drug condition that opened additional leak channels, resistance decreased to 250 MΩ while capacitance was unchanged. Students used the model to relate ion-channel conductance to the speed of voltage changes.
questions.stem: What is the initial membrane time constant?

explanations.A: Correct. τ = RmCm = (500 × 10^6 Ω)(20 × 10^-12 F) = 10 × 10^-3 s = 10 ms. This timescale describes how quickly voltage relaxes after a perturbation.
explanations.B: 1.0 ms would be a factor of ten too small.
explanations.C: 100 ms would be a factor of ten too large.
explanations.D: 10 s ignores the pico- and mega-unit conversion.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Simplified RC circuit used to model membrane charging behavior.

questions.stem: What happens to τ when leak resistance decreases to 250 MΩ?

explanations.C: Correct. With capacitance unchanged, τ is directly proportional to resistance. Halving Rm from 500 MΩ to 250 MΩ halves τ from 10 ms to 5 ms.
explanations.A: Increasing τ would require increasing resistance or capacitance.
explanations.B: τ cannot remain the same if R changes and C is constant.
explanations.D: Resistance is reduced but not eliminated, so τ does not become zero.

questions.stem: Opening additional leak channels primarily changes which circuit property?

choices.D: It decreases resistance and increases conductance.
choices.A: It increases capacitance by adding lipid bilayer.
choices.B: It removes all charge from the membrane permanently.
choices.C: It changes the battery voltage in the model.

explanations.D: Correct. Ion channels provide pathways for charge flow. Opening more leak channels increases conductance, which is the reciprocal of resistance, so the equivalent membrane resistance decreases.
explanations.A: Capacitance is mainly determined by membrane area, thickness, and dielectric properties, not the number of open channels in this simplified model.
explanations.B: The membrane can still hold charge; the voltage just relaxes faster.
explanations.C: The resting driving forces are not the same as changing the external battery voltage in this circuit model.

questions.stem: Why does a lower membrane resistance make voltage changes decay faster?

choices.A: Charge leaks across the membrane more easily.
choices.B: Lower resistance stores more charge for the same voltage.
choices.C: Lower resistance eliminates capacitance from the model.
choices.D: Lower resistance makes ions unable to move.

explanations.A: Correct. A smaller resistance provides an easier path for current, so stored membrane charge dissipates faster. Because τ = RmCm, the membrane potential returns toward rest more quickly.
explanations.B: Charge storage for a given voltage is governed by capacitance, not resistance.
explanations.C: The capacitor remains part of the model; only the leak pathway changes.
explanations.D: Lower resistance means ions move more easily, not less easily.

questions.stem: Which statement best explains why membrane capacitance is useful in this model?

choices.C: The lipid bilayer separates charge and stores electrical energy across a thin insulator.
choices.A: The membrane acts like a perfect metal wire.
choices.D: Capacitance requires ions to cross only by active transport.

explanations.C: Correct. A capacitor consists of separated charge across an insulating layer. The membrane's lipid core behaves as an insulator separating conductive intracellular and extracellular fluids, allowing voltage-dependent charge storage.
explanations.A: The lipid membrane is not a perfect conductor; that is why capacitance is meaningful.
explanations.B: Capacitance is an electrical storage property, not an enzymatic kinetic parameter.
explanations.D: Capacitive charge movement does not require ions to cross only through active transport.
